words.etymology: By adding the names of the first two letters of Georgian alphabet: ან (an) + ბან (ban). Compare ნართანიანი (nartaniani).
Probably borrowed from Russian аспира́нт (aspiránt), ultimately from Latin aspīrāns.

glosses: place where goods are very expensive
words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian апте́ка (aptéka) undergoing Georgianization to make the word pronounced more freely.
From Persian بی دولت (bi dowlat, “without wealth”).
Through Russian, ultimately from German Buchhalter.

From Old Georgian ვერაგი (veragi), ultimately from Old Armenian. See Old Georgian for more.

words.etymology: Inherited from Old Georgian კაზმვა (ḳazmva), most likely borrowed from Old Armenian կազմեմ (kazmem), from կազմ (kazm).
From Old Georgian კალამი (ḳalami), from Arabic قَلَم (qalam), from Ancient Greek κάλαμος (kálamos, “reed”).
Probably borrowed from Russian калька (kalʹka), from French calque.
From Russian классик (klassik) + -ოსი (-osi, hellenizing occupational suffix). By surface analysis, კლასიკა (ḳlasiḳa) + -ოსი (-osi).
Ultimately from Latin classicus. By surface analysis, კლასიკა (ḳlasiḳa) + -ური (-uri).

glosses: criminal (of or relating to crime or penal law)
words.etymology: From კრიმინალი (ḳriminali) + -ური (-uri), from Late Latin crīminālis.
From Old Georgian მაგალითი (magaliti), from Classical Syriac ܡܓܠܬܐ, either directly or via Old Armenian մագաղաթ (magałatʻ).

words.en: unwanted paper that has been discarded in order to be recycled
glosses: unwanted paper that has been discarded in order to be recycled

words.etymology: From present participle of ასწავლის (asc̣avlis) undergoing substantivization. The verb root determined from the now substantivized participle is *-სწავლ-ებ- (-sc̣avl-eb-), however it should be *სწავლ-ი (sc̣avl-i); this happened from contamination of the new verb form ასწავლის (asc̣avlis) with the old verb form ასწავებს (asc̣avebs).

words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian паста (pasta), ultimately from Italian pasta. The colloquial sense of “pen” is similar to the Russian slang sense of “ink for a pen, ink refill”.
Borrowed from Russian педаго́г (pedagóg), from Ancient Greek παιδαγωγός (paidagōgós).
